document_type.name: Generic Document
document_type.description: Any PDF document. Extract all visible sections, subsections, tables, lists, and content into structured JSON.

prompt: Extract document header/metadata information from this first page.
Look for ANY metadata typically found in document headers, title pages, or footers:
- Title or heading of the document
- Organisation, company, department, or agency name
1. Extract the EXACT text as it appears - do not paraphrase.
2. If a field is not found, use an empty string: ""
3. Look in: page headers, footers, title blocks, cover pages, front matter.
Return ONLY the JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):

definitions.section: A major section of the document with a distinct heading. Captures the heading text, all body paragraphs, and any nested subsections, tables, or lists within it.
definitions.unhandled_content: Content that does not clearly belong to any other section type, or boilerplate such as disclaimers, signatures, blank pages.

terms: Negotiated rate, Lump sum, fixed price, basis of charge
The basis on which the charges should be applied
Any clauses that amend the agreed rates

template: You are a document verification expert.
I am showing you {n_images} page image(s) from a document (page {page_numbers}).
INFORMATION TO VERIFY:
{information}
TASK:
Carefully examine the page image(s) and determine whether the information
above is:
- CORRECT      : The information including effective date, description, unit_price, and currency exactly or substantially matches what is
visible on the page(s). Minor formatting differences are OK.
- INCORRECT    : The information including effective date, description, unit_price, and currency is present but contains errors (wrong
values, wrong dates, wrong names, wrong amounts, etc.).
- MISSING      : The information cannot be found on the page(s) at all.
- UNVERIFIABLE : The page(s) are illegible, the query is too vague, or
you genuinely cannot determine the answer.
RULES:
1. Read ALL visible text in the image(s) carefully before deciding.
2. Compare numbers, dates, names, and amounts precisely.
3. Minor formatting differences (e.g. '01 Jan 2025' vs '1 January 2025')
are still CORRECT if the meaning is the same.
4. If the information is partially correct and partially wrong, return
INCORRECT and explain exactly what is wrong.
5. Keep your reasoning concise (1-3 sentences).
Return ONLY a JSON object:
"verdict": "CORRECT | INCORRECT | MISSING | UNVERIFIABLE",
"reasoning": "<brief explanation>"
Return the JSON now:
I am sending you {n_images} consecutive document page images.
They are numbered Image 1 through Image {n_images}.
CONTEXT:
- Document type: {document_type_name}
- Total document pages: {total_pages}
EXPECTED SECTION TYPES:
{section_definitions_json}
STEP 1 - ESTABLISH THE HEADING HIERARCHY
Before classifying anything, scan ALL images and identify the
visual hierarchy of headings by examining these cues:
VISUAL CUE            | HIGHER-LEVEL (Section)       | LOWER-LEVEL (Subsection)
Font size             | Largest                      | Smaller
Font weight           | Bold / ALL-CAPS              | Regular or lighter bold
Numbering depth       | 1, 2, 3 or I, II, III       | 1.1, 1.2, 2.1 or a, b, c
Page position         | Often starts a new page      | Continues on same page
Spacing               | Large space above/below      | Less space above/below
Indentation           | Flush left / centered        | Indented
Identify the TOP-LEVEL heading style first. Only headings matching that
top-level style are SECTIONS. Everything nested below them -- regardless
of whether it is numbered, bulleted, or has its own heading -- is a
subsection and must NOT be returned as a separate section.
STEP 2 - APPLY THE HIERARCHY TEST
For each candidate heading, ask:
(a) Does it use the SAME visual style as the top-level headings?
(b) Does it appear BETWEEN two top-level headings (i.e., nested inside a section)?
(c) Is its numbering a SUB-number of a top-level heading (e.g., 3.1 under 3)?
If (a)=YES and (b)=NO  -> it IS a section  -> include it.
If (b)=YES or (c)=YES  -> it is a subsection -> do NOT include it.
If ambiguous            -> do NOT include it (err on the side of fewer, larger sections).
COMMON TRAPS -- do NOT treat these as separate sections:
- Numbered subsections (1.1, 1.2, ...) within a numbered section (1, 2, ...)
- Bulleted or lettered lists under a heading
- Bold subheadings that are visually smaller than the true section headings
- Article/clause titles inside a legal or regulatory section
STEP 3 - CLASSIFY SPECIAL PAGES
- Cover page, title page, table of contents, executive summary, abstract,
or any introductory material before the first real section -> 'front_matter'.
- Appendices, references, bibliography, glossary, index, or any material
after the last real section -> 'back_matter'.
- Boilerplate, disclaimers, signature blocks, blank pages -> 'unhandled_content'.
STEP 4 - BUILD THE OUTPUT
RULES FOR section_name:
- Extract the EXACT heading text from the document -- do NOT paraphrase.
- Preserve original capitalisation and punctuation.
{heading_alias_rules}
- Unrecognised or ambiguous content -> section_type: 'unhandled_content'.
RULES FOR IMAGE NUMBERS:
- Use IMAGE NUMBERS (1 to {n_images}), NOT any page numbers printed on the document.
- Ignore any page numbers you see printed on the pages themselves.
- Image 1 is the first image shown, Image 2 is the second, etc.
- A section's start_image is the image where its heading first appears.
- A section's end_image is the last image before the NEXT section's heading.
- A section's image range includes ALL its subsections' images.
YOUR TASK: Identify ONLY top-level SECTIONS in the {n_images} images.
Return ONLY a JSON array (no markdown fences, no commentary):
"section_type": "one of: {section_types_csv}",
"section_name": "EXACT heading text",
"start_image": <image number, 1 to {n_images}>,
"end_image": <image number, 1 to {n_images}>,
"confidence": <0.0-1.0>
CONSTRAINTS:
- start_image and end_image MUST be between 1 and {n_images}.
- These are IMAGE NUMBERS, not printed page numbers from the document.
- All images must be covered -- no gaps, no overlaps.
- A section's image range includes ALL its subsections' images.
- Err on the side of fewer, larger sections.
- Use ONLY ASCII characters (replace unicode dashes/bullets with ASCII equivalents).
- Return valid JSON only, no markdown or extra text.
Return the JSON array now:

system_preamble: You are an expert data extraction agent. Your ONLY job is to copy text EXACTLY as it appears in the document images.
=== ABSOLUTE RULES (never violate) ===
1. VERBATIM COPY: Every word, number, and punctuation mark must match the source document exactly.
- Do NOT fix spelling mistakes, grammatical errors, or inconsistent capitalisation.
- Do NOT rephrase, paraphrase, summarise, condense, or reword anything.
- Do NOT add words, articles, or connectors that are not visible in the document.
- Do NOT drop words, even if they seem redundant or repeated.
2. CHARACTER ENCODING: Output ONLY ASCII characters (codes 0-127).
- Replace unicode dashes (em-dash, en-dash) with ' - ' (space-hyphen-space).
- Replace bullet characters (bullet, triangles, arrows) with '- ' (hyphen-space).
- Replace curly/smart quotes with straight quotes.
- Replace ellipsis character with '...'.
- For all other non-ASCII characters (e.g. degree signs, trademark, copyright, accented letters): keep the closest ASCII equivalent or transliteration.
- NEVER silently delete a character; always replace with an ASCII equivalent.
3. COMPLETENESS: Extract ALL visible text from EVERY page shown. Do not skip or abbreviate.
- Scan each page from top to bottom, left to right.
- If content looks repetitive (e.g. similar clauses, table rows), extract ALL occurrences.
- If text is partially obscured but readable, extract what is visible.
- If text is completely illegible, use '[illegible]'.
4. JSON OUTPUT:
- Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown fences, no commentary, no preamble.
- Start your response with {{ or [.
- Empty fields: '' for strings, [] for arrays, {{}} for objects.
- Never use null, 'N/A', 'None', 'Unknown', or any placeholder text.
- Escape special characters properly: backslash, quotes, newlines within strings.
Schema to follow:
{schema}
default_template: Extract ALL text from the document pages shown into the JSON schema above.
Section: {section_name} ({section_type})
Pages: {start_page} to {end_page}
Next section after this one: '{next_section_name}'
{image_info}
{doc_type_guidance}
=== WHAT TO EXTRACT ===
Extract ALL visible text from page {start_page} through page {end_page}.
STOP extracting when you reach content that belongs to '{next_section_name}'.
=== HEADING ===
- 'heading': The EXACT section heading text as a plain string, copied verbatim.
- 'heading_level': The nesting depth: '1' for top-level, '2' for sub-section, '3' for sub-sub, etc.
=== BODY (critical — read carefully) ===
The 'body' field is an array of strings. Each string represents one LOGICAL BLOCK:
- A paragraph (one or more lines of continuous prose) = ONE string.
If a sentence wraps across multiple visual lines, join them into one string.
Preserve the paragraph's exact wording.
- A numbered or lettered clause (e.g. '(a) ...', '(i) ...', '1.1.1 ...') = ONE string per clause.
Include the number/letter prefix as part of the string.
If a clause spans multiple visual lines, join them into one string.
- A bullet point or dash-prefixed item = ONE string per item.
Include the bullet/dash prefix as part of the string.
Do NOT extract bullets as table rows.
- A standalone line (e.g. a short note, a label, a date) = ONE string.
RULE: When in doubt whether two lines are one block or two, keep them SEPARATE.
It is better to have too many strings than to accidentally merge unrelated content.
=== SUBSECTIONS ===
If you see sub-headings inside this section, nest them as objects in the 'subsections' array.
Each subsection has its own 'heading', 'heading_level', 'body', 'subsections', and 'tables'.
Apply the same body extraction rules recursively.
=== TABLES ===
- Extract every table: 'caption' (if any, otherwise ''), 'headers' (array of column header strings), 'rows' (array of arrays).
- Each row is an array of cell strings, matching the column order.
- Copy cell text exactly. For merged cells that span columns, place the text in the first cell and use '' for the spanned cells.
- For merged cells that span rows, repeat the text in each row the cell spans.
- If a cell contains multiple lines, join them with a single space.
=== IMAGES ===
- For images: match by PAGE and POSITION from the image list above.
- Copy the EXACT path into the 'image' field.
- Include any visible captions in the 'text' field.
=== MULTI-PAGE SECTIONS ===
- Process ALL pages from {start_page} to {end_page} sequentially.
- Content that flows from one page to the next is part of the same logical block.
If a paragraph or clause starts on one page and continues on the next page,
join them into a single string (do not split mid-sentence).
- Page headers and footers (e.g. page numbers, document titles repeated on every page) should be EXCLUDED.
=== SELF-CHECK (do this before returning) ===
Before returning your JSON, mentally verify:
1. Have I extracted text from EVERY page ({start_page} through {end_page})?
2. Have I included ALL paragraphs, clauses, bullet points, and table rows?
3. Is every string a verbatim copy (no rewording, no missing words)?
4. Did I stop before content belonging to '{next_section_name}'?
Return the complete JSON now (start with {{ or [, no markdown):
general_rules: - Every word must be copied verbatim from the document. Do not rephrase.
- Do not skip repetitive content. If ten similar clauses exist, extract all ten.
- Numbered items like (a), (b), (c) or (i), (ii), (iii) are separate body strings, not one merged string.
- Do not invent section headings or field values that are not visible in the document.
- Check every page: did you extract all content from pages {start_page} through {end_page}?

review_template: You are a document quality reviewer. You are provided with:
1. PDF PAGE IMAGES -- the ground truth for section '{section_name}'
2. EXTRACTED TEXT pulled from those pages
TASK -- compare the extracted text against the images and report:
1. WORD_ACCURACY: words in the extracted text that do not match the image.
Accuracy = (1 - mismatched/total_words)*100. >=95% high, >=85% medium, else low.
2. MISSING_INFORMATION_ACCURACY: words visible in the image but absent from extracted text.
Accuracy = (1 - missing/total_words)*100. >=95% high, >=85% medium, else low.
3. DUPLICATION_ACCURACY: entire sections that are repeated (not individual words).
Count >=1 duplicated sections -> low; else high.

template: You are a document analysis expert.
I will give you:
1. A list of TERMS to search for.
2. SUMMARIES of all sections extracted from a document.
TERMS:
{terms_json}
DOCUMENT SECTIONS:
{section_summaries}
TASK:
For each term, identify which document sections are related to it.
A section is 'related' if it discusses, defines, references,
or is materially relevant to the term -- even indirectly.
RELEVANCE LEVELS:
- high: The section directly addresses, defines, or is primarily about the term.
- medium: The section contains meaningful references or provisions related to the term.
- low: The section has indirect or tangential relevance to the term.
RULES:
- Only include sections with genuine relevance. Do NOT force-match.
- If no section is related to a term, return an empty list for that term.
- Use the EXACT section names as provided in the summaries.
- Provide a brief, specific reason for each match.
- Provide an evidence list, all EXACT source text for each match.
Return ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):
"related_sections": [
"section_name": "<exact section name from above>",
"evidence_list": "[<list of exact text from the source>]",
"relevance": "high | medium | low",
"reason": "<brief explanation of why this section relates to the term>"
Return the JSON now:
You are a contract analysis expert.
I will give you the extracted sections of a document.
Your task is to identify the EFFECTIVE DATE -- the date from
which the contract or variation should apply.
{header_context}
DOCUMENT SECTIONS:
{section_summaries}
WHAT TO LOOK FOR (in priority order):
1. An explicitly stated 'Effective Date', 'Commencement Date',
or 'Start Date' defined in the contract terms.
2. The execution or signing date of the contract or variation.
3. A date derived from context -- e.g. 'this Deed takes effect
from the Commencement Date defined in clause X', or a date
that can be read from a variation's terms.
4. Any date that indicates when the agreement's terms begin
to apply.
DATE TYPES (use these labels):
- effective_date: Explicitly labelled as 'Effective Date'
- commencement_date: A 'Commencement Date' or 'Start Date'
- execution_date: The date the document was signed/executed
- variation_date: The date a variation takes effect
- other: Any other date that serves as the effective date
Return ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):
"primary_effective_date": {{
"date": "<date as written in the document>",
"normalised": "<YYYY-MM-DD format, or empty if ambiguous>",
"source_section": "<section name where found>",
"date_type": "<one of the types above>",
"confidence": "high | medium | low",
"reason": "<brief explanation>"
"all_dates_found": [
"date": "<date text>",
"normalised": "<YYYY-MM-DD or empty>",
"source_section": "<section name>",
"date_type": "<type>",
"confidence": "high | medium | low",
"reason": "<why this could be the effective date>"
"no_date_found": false
RULES:
- primary_effective_date = the MOST LIKELY effective date.
- all_dates_found = ALL candidate dates, even low confidence.
- If no date can be identified, set no_date_found to true.
- Use the EXACT date text as it appears in the document.
- normalised = ISO YYYY-MM-DD where possible.
Return the JSON now:
You are a contract pricing analysis expert.
I will give you the extracted sections of a contract document.
Your task is to identify every UNIT OF MEASURE (UOM) used for
pricing in this contract.
DOCUMENT SECTIONS:
{section_summaries}
WHAT TO LOOK FOR:
- Rate tables, pricing schedules, or fee schedules that specify
how charges are measured (e.g. per hour, per day, each, etc.)
- Contract clauses that define how pricing is calculated
- Column headers in tables (e.g. 'Rate ($/hr)', 'Unit', 'UOM')
- Pricing descriptions (e.g. 'Lump Sum', 'Per Tonne', 'Monthly')
- Any reference to the basis on which charges are applied
COMMON UNITS OF MEASURE:
- Time-based: Hourly, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Annually
- Quantity-based: Each, Per Item, Per Unit, Per Lot
- Weight/Volume: Per Tonne, Per Kilogram, Per Litre, Per Cubic Metre
- Distance/Area: Per Metre, Per Square Metre, Per Kilometre
- Fixed: Lump Sum, Fixed Fee, Flat Rate
- Other: Per Trip, Per Mobilisation, Per Shift, Percentage
Return ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, start with {{):
"units_of_measure": [
"unit": "<UOM as written in the document>",
"normalised_unit": "<standardised lowercase label, e.g. hour, day, each, lump_sum, tonne>",
"applies_to": "<what this unit prices -- e.g. Labour rates, Equipment hire, Materials>",
"source_section": "<section name where found>",
"confidence": "high | medium | low",
"verbatim_text": "<the exact text/phrase from the document where this UOM appears>"
"distinct_units": ["<list of unique normalised_unit values>"],
"no_uom_found": false
RULES:
- Include EVERY distinct UOM reference, even if the same unit
appears in multiple sections (list each occurrence).
- distinct_units = deduplicated list of normalised_unit values.
- If no pricing or UOM information exists, set no_uom_found to true.
- Use the EXACT text as it appears for 'unit' and 'verbatim_text'.
- normalised_unit = short, lowercase, standardised label.
Return the JSON now:

image_description_prompt: Analyze this image from the '{section_name}' section.
Provide a SPECIFIC description (max {description_len} words) with any visible text or colours.

image_context_hints._default: Describe this image briefly, noting any text, labels, or key visual elements.
